1.exposition: The soaring Christology of chapter one now lands as a summons. Because the Son so far outshines the angels, the writer draws his first practical inference — a therefore that turns doctrine into duty: we must pay the closer attention to what we have heard, lest we drift away from it. The peril he names is not violent apostasy but a slow, careless slippage, a ship carried past its harbour. Then comes the letter's first argument from the lesser to the greater: if the law spoken through angels was so binding that every transgression met its just penalty, how shall we escape if we neglect so great a salvation? That salvation carries an unimpeachable pedigree — first announced by the Lord himself, confirmed by those who heard him, and sealed by God's own witness in signs, wonders, miracles, and gifts of the Holy Spirit. The greater the message, the graver the guilt of drifting past it.
1.original_language: Two verbs set the tone. Prosechein (v.1) means to hold the mind to a thing, to attend the more closely; its opposite is pararyōmen, a nautical word for a ship that slips past the harbour mouth or a stream that flows away — drift, not defiance, is the danger. The sin of v.3 is amelēsantes (“having been careless of, made light of”), again a sin of neglect rather than open revolt. The law's penalty is an endikon misthapodosian, a “just recompense,” wages exactly earned; and in v.4 God is synepimartyrountos — literally “co-testifying,” adding his signature alongside the apostles' word.
1.historical_context: The premise of “the word spoken by angels” (v.2) rests on a widespread Second-Temple tradition that the Torah was mediated at Sinai through angelic hosts (Acts 7:53; Galatians 3:19) — a dignity the writer grants only to argue from it: if mediated law bound so firmly, the Son's direct word binds far more. The chain of attestation — Lord, then eyewitnesses, then God confirming with signs — meets the synagogue's own standard that a matter stands on multiple reliable witnesses (Deuteronomy 19:15). And the drifting image would strike readers who knew the Mediterranean: an inattentive pilot lets the current carry his vessel past the safe anchorage, with no easy return against wind and tide.
1.christ: The salvation in danger of neglect is one that began to be spoken by the Lord himself — no longer a word relayed through intermediaries, but the direct utterance of the Son who is greater than every angel. The logic of the whole warning is Christological: the surpassing dignity of the Speaker measures the weight of the message and the danger of drifting past him. To make light of this gospel is to despise not merely a doctrine but the very Person the angels worship.

verses.note: The “therefore” carries the whole freight of chapter one: because the Son is so great, we ought to give the more earnest heed to the things we have heard. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson press prosechein — to fasten the mind more closely — set against pararyōmen, to slip or drift away like a boat past its moorings. puritan Matthew Henry names the peril exactly: the great danger is not chiefly bold rejection but inattention — truths heard yet unheeded quietly slip away, and the careless soul is carried past its anchorage.

The lesser term of the argument: the word spoken by angels proved steadfast, and every transgression and disobedience received a just recompense. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson take “the word spoken by angels” as the law given through angelic mediation (Acts 7:53; Galatians 3:19) — firm, valid, and exact in its penalties. reformed Calvin notes the sanctions were sure: under that law no breach went unrequited. The point is a fortiori, from the smaller to the greater — and the greater is coming.

The searching question that has no answer: how shall we escape if we neglect so great a salvation? grammatical-historical Barnes drives the logic home — there is no other way of salvation, so its neglect leaves no escape; and the sin is amelēsantes, mere careless disregard, not open enmity. reformed Gill reads the rhetorical question as bearing the certainty of judgment: those who slight so great a salvation cannot evade God's righteous sentence. Its greatness is threefold — greater in its Author (the Son), its blessings, and its cost — and it “began to be spoken by the Lord” himself, then was “confirmed to us by those who heard him,” the apostolic eyewitnesses.

God adds his own signature to the testimony: bearing witness with signs and wonders and various miracles and gifts of the Holy Spirit, distributed according to his will. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the compound verb — God “co-testifies” alongside the apostles — and the threefold display of supernatural power that authenticated the message and left the hearers without excuse. reformed Calvin adds that the cumulative attestation seals the word as God's own, so that to neglect it is to despise the witness of God himself.

witnesses.point: Prosechein means to attend the more closely; the danger is pararyōmen — a gradual, careless drifting past the truth, not open apostasy.
The great danger is inattention: truths heard but not heeded slip away, and the careless soul drifts past its anchorage.
If the law mediated by angels punished every breach, far graver is the guilt of neglecting the greater salvation spoken by the Son.
The rhetorical “how shall we escape” bears the weight of certainty — no escape from God's righteous judgment for those who neglect so great a salvation.

external.name: William L. Lane
external.point: The passage is the first of the sermon's several “words of exhortation,” pastoral interruptions aimed at a wavering community — the a fortiori argument presses the readers not to let the confession they received slip from their grasp.

1.application: Drifting needs no decision; it only needs neglect. A boat left untended does not steer for the rocks — it simply follows the current. So the first danger the letter names is not that we would renounce Christ, but that we would quietly stop paying attention.

5.pericope_label: The Son Made Lower than Angels
5.exposition: The argument takes a surprising turn. Having exalted the Son above the angels, the writer now stoops with him beneath them — for it was not to angels that God subjected the world to come, but to man. He proves it from Psalm 8: what is man that you are mindful of him? — made a little lower than the angels, yet crowned with glory and honour and set over the works of God's hands, with all things put under his feet. Then he concedes the obvious: “we do not yet see everything subjected to him.” The destiny is real, the fulfilment invisible — until the eye is redirected. But we see Jesus, made lower than the angels for the suffering of death, now crowned with glory and honour, that by God's grace he might taste death for everyone. The one man in whom Psalm 8 has already come true is the crucified and enthroned Son — the cross itself his coronation.
5.original_language: The pivotal phrase brachy ti (v.7) is deliberately ambiguous: it can mean “a little” in degree (lower in rank than the angels) or “a little” in time (“for a little while”) — and the writer exploits both, reading of humanity's rank in the Psalm and of the Son's brief humiliation in v.9. “We see Jesus” answers the “we do not yet see” of v.8 — sight set against sight. To “taste death” (geuomai thanatou) is to experience it fully, not merely to sip it. And here lies a famous textual crux: whether Christ tasted death chariti theou (“by the grace of God”) or, as an ancient variant known to Origen read, chōris theou (“apart from God”).
5.historical_context: Psalm 8's “not yet” was a live problem for Jewish messianism. The Davidic king was expected to restore Israel's supremacy and break her enemies; “we do not yet see all things subjected” states the gap between that hope and the visible world with painful plainness. Hebrews answers it not by argument but by sight: look at Jesus. And the claim was scandalous in its Greco-Roman setting, for crucifixion was Rome's most shameful death — Cicero called it “the most wretched of deaths.” To present that very death as the ground on which Jesus is “crowned with glory and honour” is a stark honour-shame reversal: the cross read as the throne-room.
5.christ: “We see Jesus” is the letter's first naming of the name, and it is the key that unlocks Psalm 8. What was written of humanity's destiny — made a little lower, then crowned and given dominion — is fulfilled in the one who became human to fulfil it. The single verse compresses the whole Christ-event: made lower (incarnation), the suffering of death (the cross), crowned with glory and honour (resurrection and exaltation). The dominion Adam lost and mankind does not yet exercise is already realized, in principle and in pledge, in the crucified and enthroned Son.

verses.note: The theme resumes: it was not to angels that God subjected the world to come. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson take “the world to come” as the new Messianic order Christ inaugurates — the age of the gospel and the Son's kingdom — governed not by angels but, as the Psalm will show, by man in the person of the Son. eastern Chrysostom and puritan Matthew Henry note the pointed “of which we are speaking”: this coming world is the very subject of the epistle.

The proof-text, cited in the writer's manner without naming author or place: what is man, that you are mindful of him, or the son of man, that you care for him? (Psalm 8:4). grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson hear the psalmist's wonder that the great God should regard frail, lowly man at all. The writer reads the words on two levels — of man in general, whom God so dignified, and pre-eminently of the Son of Man, in whom that dignity is realized (v.9).

You made him a little lower than the angels; you crowned him with glory and honour and set him over the works of your hands. grammatical-historical Robertson flags the crucial brachy ti — “a little lower” in rank, or “for a little while” lower — the very ambiguity the writer will turn upon Christ's temporary humiliation. reformed Calvin dwells on the crowning: the dignity and dominion God conferred on man at creation (Genesis 1:28), lost in Adam and awaiting recovery.

You have put all things in subjection under his feet — and the writer presses the word “all”: in subjecting everything, God “left nothing that is not put under him.” reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical Barnes mark the completeness of the grant, which excepts nothing. “But now we do not yet see everything subjected to him”: in fact man exercises no such universal dominion — the promise stands unrealized, the gap between the word and the world left open, to be filled by Jesus in the next verse.

The gap is closed by a person: we see Jesus, made a little lower than the angels for the suffering of death, crowned with glory and honour. grammatical-historical Barnes: what we do not see fulfilled in man at large we do see fulfilled in Jesus, exalted as the reward of his suffering. Two cruxes divide the witnesses. On the text, Barnes, Robertson, and grammatical-historical Ellicott keep “by the grace of God” (chariti theou) and reject the ancient variant “apart from God” (chōris theou) noted by Origen. On the extent of tasting death “for everyone,” they part sharply: wesleyan-arminian Wesley and Clarke, with Barnes, read a universal atonement — Christ died for all without exception — while reformed Calvin restricts “everyone” to the faithful, the “many sons” of v.10; Robertson recalls the old mediating formula, “sufficient for all, efficient for some.”

witnesses.point: What we do not see fulfilled in humanity at large we see fulfilled in Jesus; and “taste death for everyone” affirms a general atonement in the plainest terms.
Restricts “taste death for everyone” to the faithful — the many sons brought to glory — not every individual without exception.
Reads “taste death for everyone” as universal: by the grace of God, Christ died for every man without exception.
Notes the ancient variant “apart from God” (chōris theou) known to Origen, but rejects it for the received reading “by the grace of God.”

external.name: F.F. Bruce
external.point: “We see Jesus” turns the reader from the not-yet of universal subjection to the already of the crowned and crucified Jesus, in whom the destiny Psalm 8 assigns to humanity is realized ahead of the rest.

5.application: Faith often lives in the space between the promise and the plain evidence — “we do not yet see.” Hebrews does not resolve that tension by pretending the dominion is already visible; it resolves it by redirecting the gaze. When the world will not show you the kingdom, the answer is not to squint harder at the world, but to look at Jesus.

10.exposition: Why should the Son of glory die? Because it was fitting. It became God — for whom and by whom are all things — in bringing many sons to glory, to perfect the captain of their salvation through sufferings. The cross is no accident or embarrassment but the fitness of God's own plan: no suffering, no solidarity; no solidarity, no sympathetic priest. The Sanctifier and the sanctified are all of one, so the exalted Son is not ashamed to call them brethren — a kinship proved from Psalm 22 and Isaiah 8. Because the children share flesh and blood, he took the same, that through death he might destroy the one who holds the power of death — the devil — and free those enslaved all their lives by the fear of death. He came to help not angels but the seed of Abraham; therefore he was made like his brethren in every way, to be a merciful and faithful high priest who makes propitiation for sins, and — himself tempted in his suffering — able to help those who are tempted. Here the letter's grand theme, the priesthood of the Son, is first sounded, grounded in the very flesh he shares with us.
10.original_language: Eprepen (v.10, “it was fitting, becoming”) makes the cross a matter of divine propriety, not bare necessity. The Son is the archēgos — “pioneer, founder, captain,” the one who goes first and opens the way — and he is perfected (teleiōsai) through suffering: not made morally better, for he was ever sinless, but brought to his full completion as Saviour, wholly qualified for his office. In v.14 he acts to destroy (katargēsē, “render powerless, bring to nought”) the devil; in v.17 his work is hilaskesthai — to make propitiation, to atone; and in v.18 he is able boēthēsai, literally to “run to the cry” of the tempted — timely help for those who call out.
10.historical_context: The archēgos of v.10 carried rich resonance in the Greco-Roman world: the founder-hero of a city, or the leader who goes ahead of his people through danger to open a path they can then follow — a Heracles clearing the monsters, a captain first into the breach. Christ's solidarity with “the seed of Abraham” (v.16) frames the incarnation covenantally: the Son enters the family of God's promise to Abraham (Galatians 3:29). And the note of “lifelong bondage to the fear of death” (v.15) speaks to an ancient world in which the terror of dying, and of what might lie beyond, held whole populations in dread.
10.christ: Every strand here is Christological. The archēgos goes ahead through suffering to open the way of glory for those who follow, and is “perfected” not in virtue but in vocation — through the full human experience of temptation and death he becomes completely the high priest he was appointed to be. The incarnation is no reluctant concession but a deliberate identification: he took flesh and blood, called us brethren, shared our trials, tasted our death — so that his priesthood should be exercised not over us from a distance but among us, from the inside. The chapter that began with the Son far above the angels ends with the Son beside the tempted.

verses.note: It became God, for whom and by whom are all things, to make the captain of their salvation perfect through sufferings. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson stress the word “fitting”: a suffering Saviour perfectly suited the character of God, the end and author of all. eastern Cyril of Alexandria takes the “perfecting” of the archēgos not morally — for the Son was ever sinless — but as his consummation as Saviour, fully fitted for his saving office through what he endured. puritan Matthew Henry sees the whole verse forestalling the scandal of the cross: the death of the Prince of salvation was fitting, not shameful, for by it he brings “many sons to glory.”

He who sanctifies and those who are sanctified are all of one — one origin, one nature, one stock. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson locate here the ground of the brotherhood: having taken human nature, Christ is truly one with those he makes holy. eastern Cyril marvels at the sequel — “he is not ashamed to call them brethren” — the exalted Son stooping to own his people as kin, an act of wonderful condescension and love.

The first proof from Scripture: I will declare your name to my brethren; in the midst of the congregation I will sing your praise (Psalm 22:22). grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the source — the great psalm of the cross, whose opening cry Jesus made his own — in which the sufferer calls his people “brethren.” eastern Cyril hears Christ standing amid the assembly as its chief singer, one with his people in making the Father known and in worship.

Two further citations from Isaiah 8:17–18: I will put my trust in him; and again, Behold, I and the children God has given me. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson observe that in “I will put my trust in him” Christ takes the believer's own posture of faith — as man he leans on God, sharing our life of dependence. eastern Cyril reads the second line as the Son presenting himself with “the children God gave him,” one household gathered about him — kinship in faith and in family alike.

Since the children share in flesh and blood, he likewise partook of the same, that through death he might destroy the one who holds the power of death — that is, the devil. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson ground the incarnation in this solidarity: because his people are human, the Son took real humanity. reformed Calvin marks the strange strategy of the victory — by his own dying, Christ renders powerless (katargēsē) the one who wielded death. eastern Cyril names the foe: Satan, who held men in his grip through sin and death, is defeated by Christ within his own domain.

The purpose of that conquest is deliverance: to free those who through fear of death were all their lifetime subject to bondage. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson describe the tyranny — the dread of dying, with its terror of judgment and the unknown, holds men in a lifelong slavery they cannot break. reformed Calvin adds that this deliverance is a fruit of the atonement: knowing death conquered, the believer is loosed from its fear. The chain is broken not by denying death but by defeating it.

A verse the witnesses read two ways: for surely it is not angels he helps, but the seed of Abraham. reformed Gill and Calvin follow the older rendering, “he took not on him the nature of angels, but the seed of Abraham” — Christ assumed human, not angelic, nature; Calvin presses this against the docetists, that Christ was real man of human stock. grammatical-historical Barnes, Ellicott, and Robertson, with eastern Chrysostom and wesleyan-arminian Clarke, prefer “he takes hold” (epilambanetai) — to lay hold of in order to rescue: Christ seizes fallen man to save him, and lets the fallen angels fall. Chrysostom pictures man fleeing and Christ pursuing to catch him. Either way the wonder is the same — that this love passed by the angels for the seed of Abraham (cf. Galatians 3:29).

The necessary conclusion: in all things he had to be made like his brethren, that he might be a merciful and faithful high priest in things pertaining to God, to make propitiation for the sins of the people. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the “in all things” (sin excepted): full likeness to us was necessary to his office. reformed Calvin marks the great theme now opened — the priesthood — for which his humanity fits him: merciful toward men, faithful toward God. The verb hilaskesthai names the priestly work itself — to make atonement, propitiation, for sin.

The chapter's pastoral close: because he himself suffered when tempted, he is able to help those who are being tempted. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson ground his sympathy in real experience — Christ genuinely suffered under trial and so knows it from within. puritan Matthew Henry draws the comfort: the tempted Saviour is the able helper of the tempted, and boēthēsai — to run to the aid of one who cries out — promises timely, present relief. The high priest is no distant figure superintending our struggle from above, but one who has been through it.

witnesses.point: The “perfecting” of the archēgos is not moral but vocational — his consummation as Saviour through suffering — and the exalted Son is “not ashamed to call them brethren.”
By his own death Christ renders the devil powerless; and his full likeness to his brethren fits him to be the merciful and faithful high priest.
Reads v.16 as Christ “taking hold” to rescue — as man fled, Christ pursued and laid hold of him, passing by the angels for the seed of Abraham.
The tempted Saviour is the able helper of the tempted; his own suffering under trial makes his sympathy real and his help timely.

external.name: Peter T. O'Brien
external.point: Christ's solidarity with humanity — sharing flesh and blood, tempted in his suffering — is precisely what qualifies him as the merciful and faithful high priest; his “perfection” through suffering is vocational, the Son fully equipped for the priestly office the letter will now expound.

10.application: The comfort of this chapter is not a Saviour who explains our suffering from a safe height, but one who entered it — sharing our flesh, our trials, our dying. Whatever tempts you today, you are not met by a stranger. The High Priest has been where you are, and he runs to the cry of those who call.

_meta.title: So Great a Salvation
reflection: The first danger the letter names is not rejection but drift — to “neglect so great a salvation” by simply ceasing to pay attention (2:1–3). Where in your walk are you drifting, not by decision but by inattention?

When the world does not yet show the kingdom, Hebrews answers, “we do not yet see… but we see Jesus” (2:8–9). What “not yet” are you staring at, and what would change if you fixed your eyes on the crowned and crucified Jesus instead?

Christ shared our flesh and was tempted in his suffering so that “he is able to help those who are being tempted” (v.18). What temptation would you bring to a High Priest who has been through it himself, rather than carry it alone?
